suggestions.content:
• Rapid prototypes flip validation vs. requirements, creating downstream ambiguity.
  • Legacy guardrails (security reviews, SOW templates) assume waterfall intake.
  • Stakeholders love the speed, but prompts/scope/debt handoffs are inconsistent.
  • Life Sciences work adds GxP/HIPAA scrutiny missing from prototypes.

Recommendations

  • Codify an end-to-end process with explicit gates and owners.
  • Capture portable evidence (prompt logs, validation notes) during prototyping.
  • Add assessment scorecards + debt inventories before committing estimates.
  • Align delivery/compliance/commercial teams on AI-assisted review & pricing.
  • Phase 0 suitability matrix to vet vibe coding fit.
  • Phase 1 prototype boundaries + prompt engineering dossier.
  • Phase 2 technical assessment with debt register and go/no-go guidance.
  • Phase 3-6 templates for requirements, ADRs, hardening, estimation, governance.

Example accelerators

  • UI vs. integration suitability grid.
  • Prompt session log + client validation record.
  • Technical assessment scorecard + Definition of Done checklist.
  • GxP addendum & AI usage declaration for regulated spaces.

<GridDesigner template="dual-panel">
  <GridArea area="left" contentType="primary-title" importance="critical">
    <ContentRenderer type="primary-title" content="What we learned & observed" />
  </GridArea>
  
  <GridArea area="left" contentType="criteria-list" importance="important">
    <ContentRenderer 
      type="criteria-list" 
      content={[
        "Rapid prototypes flip validation vs. requirements, creating downstream ambiguity.",
        "Legacy guardrails (security reviews, SOW templates) assume waterfall intake.",
        "Stakeholders love the speed, but prompts/scope/debt handoffs are inconsistent.",
        "Life Sciences work adds GxP/HIPAA scrutiny missing from prototypes."
      ]} 
    />
  </GridArea>
  
  <GridArea area="right" contentType="secondary-title" importance="important">
    <ContentRenderer type="secondary-title" content="Suggested framework components" />
  </GridArea>
  
  <GridArea area="right" contentType="criteria-list" importance="important">
    <ContentRenderer 
      type="criteria-list" 
      content={[
        "Phase 0 suitability matrix to vet vibe coding fit.",
        "Phase 1 prototype boundaries + prompt engineering dossier.",
        "Phase 2 technical assessment with debt register and go/no-go guidance.",
        "Phase 3-6 templates for requirements, ADRs, hardening, estimation, governance."
      ]} 
    />
  </GridArea>
  
  <GridArea area="right" contentType="secondary-title" importance="supporting">
    <ContentRenderer type="secondary-title" content="Example accelerators" />
  </GridArea>
  
  <GridArea area="right" contentType="criteria-list" importance="supporting">
    <ContentRenderer 
      type="criteria-list"

        "UI vs. integration suitability grid.",
        "Prompt session log + client validation record.",
        "Technical assessment scorecard + Definition of Done checklist.",
        "GxP addendum & AI usage declaration for regulated spaces."
      ]} 
    />
  </GridArea>
</GridDesigner>

suggestions.content:
Rapid AI prototyping has transformed our speed to market, but created critical gaps:

  • Legacy processes assume waterfall requirements gathering
  • Security and compliance reviews happen after the fact
  • Technical debt accumulates without visibility
  • Handoffs between prototype and delivery teams are inconsistent

Life Sciences projects add GxP/HIPAA complexity that prototypes never consider.

A structured approach that maintains velocity while adding enterprise guardrails:

  • Phase 0: Suitability assessment for vibe coding fit
  • Phase 1: Bounded prototyping with prompt documentation
  • Phase 2: Technical debt assessment and go/no-go gates
  • Phase 3-6: Templates for requirements, ADRs, hardening, and governance

Portable evidence capture ensures nothing gets lost in translation.

Quantifiable improvements across the delivery lifecycle:

  • Reduce requirements ambiguity by 60% through structured validation
  • Cut technical debt surprises by 40% with early assessment
  • Accelerate SOW generation by 30% with standardized templates
  • Improve client satisfaction through transparent AI usage disclosure

Aligned commercial, delivery, and compliance teams on AI-assisted workflows.

suggestions.content:
<MatrixGrid columns={2}>
  <MatrixCell 
    label="Phase 0: Suitability Assessment" 
    result="Strategic Fit Analysis"
    tone="positive"
  >
    Evaluate project characteristics against vibe coding criteria. UI-heavy projects with clear business logic score highest. Complex integration or regulated environments require additional scrutiny.
  </MatrixCell>
  
  <MatrixCell 
    label="Phase 1: Prototype Boundaries" 
    result="Structured Exploration"
    tone="positive"
  >
    Define scope limits, capture prompt engineering sessions, document client validation in real-time. Create portable evidence dossier for downstream teams.
  </MatrixCell>
  
  <MatrixCell 
    label="Phase 2: Technical Assessment" 
    result="Go/No-Go Decision"
    tone="neutral"
  >
    Systematic evaluation of technical debt, security implications, and scalability requirements. Quantify effort to production-ready state.
  </MatrixCell>
  
  <MatrixCell 
    label="Phase 3-6: Enterprise Delivery" 
    result="Standardized Templates"
    tone="positive"
  >
    Requirements derivation, architecture decisions, hardening checklists, testing strategy, effort estimation, and SOW templates optimized for AI-assisted development.
  </MatrixCell>
  
  <MatrixCell 
    label="Key Accelerators" 
    result="Reusable Assets"
    tone="positive"
  >
    UI vs. integration suitability grid, prompt session logs, technical scorecards, Definition of Done checklists, GxP addendums for regulated industries.
  </MatrixCell>
  
  <MatrixCell 
    label="Quality Gates" 
    result="Explicit Checkpoints"
    tone="neutral"
  >
    Phase transition criteria with clear owners and deliverables. Risk assessment at each gate with escalation paths for blockers.
  </MatrixCell>
</MatrixGrid>

suggestions.content:
<Timeline
  items={[
    {
      title: "Phase 0: Strategic Assessment",
      subtitle: "Week 0-1 | Stakeholder: Solution Architect",
      description: "Evaluate project suitability for vibe coding using UI/integration matrix. Assess regulatory requirements (GxP/HIPAA). Document business value and risk tolerance. Output: Go/No-Go decision with rationale."
    },
    {
      title: "Phase 1: Bounded Prototyping",
      subtitle: "Week 1-3 | Stakeholder: AI Developer + Client",
      description: "Define prototype scope boundaries. Capture all prompt sessions and iterations. Document client validation in real-time. Create portable evidence dossier. Output: Working prototype with complete prompt log."
    },
    {
      title: "Phase 2: Technical Assessment",
      subtitle: "Week 3-4 | Stakeholder: Technical Lead",
      description: "Systematic evaluation of code quality, security implications, and technical debt. Create quantified assessment scorecard. Define production readiness requirements. Output: Go/No-Go for enterprise delivery."
    },
    {
      title: "Phase 3: Requirements Derivation",
      subtitle: "Week 4-5 | Stakeholder: Business Analyst",
      description: "Translate prototype capabilities into formal requirements. Document assumptions and constraints. Create user stories with acceptance criteria. Output: Requirements specification with traceability to prototype."
    },
    {
      title: "Phase 4: Architecture & Design",
      subtitle: "Week 5-6 | Stakeholder: Solution Architect",
      description: "Define enterprise-scale architecture. Document integration points and data flows. Create Architecture Decision Records (ADRs). Output: Technical design documentation."
    },
    {
      title: "Phase 5: Production Hardening",
      subtitle: "Week 6-8 | Stakeholder: Development Team",
      description: "Implement security controls, error handling, and monitoring. Complete production hardening checklist. Execute testing strategy. Output: Production-ready application."
    },
    {
      title: "Phase 6: Governance & Commercial",
      subtitle: "Week 8-9 | Stakeholder: Delivery Lead + Commercial",
      description: "Finalize SOW with AI usage disclosure. Complete compliance documentation. Define pricing model based on actual effort. Output: Signed engagement with clear deliverables."
    }
  ]}
/>

suggestions.content:
| Criteria | Key Question | Why It Matters |
|----------|--------------|----------------|
| **Problem Fit** | Is this problem suitable for rapid prototyping? | Not all problems benefit from vibe coding |
| **Client Readiness** | Can the client engage in rapid iteration? | Requires available stakeholders |
| **Complexity Assessment** | UI-heavy or logic-heavy? | Vibe coding excels at UI, struggles with complex logic |
| **Integration Landscape** | How many systems must this connect to? | Integration complexity is often underestimated |
| **Regulatory Environment** | What compliance requirements exist? | GxP, HIPAA, SOX introduce extra gates |

suggestions.content:
<MatrixGrid columns={2}>
  <MatrixCell label="High UI · Low Integration" result="✅ Ideal for vibe coding" tone="positive">
    Ship the full clickable experience and document decisions early.
  </MatrixCell>
  <MatrixCell label="High UI · High Integration" result="⚠️ Prototype UI only" tone="caution">
    Pair with integration spikes so downstream systems are not underestimated.
  </MatrixCell>
  <MatrixCell label="High Logic · Low Integration" result="⚠️ Careful scoping" tone="caution">
    Validate business logic early and keep the prototype intentionally shallow.
  </MatrixCell>
  <MatrixCell label="High Logic · High Integration" result="❌ Traditional approach recommended" tone="negative">
    Use a classic discovery + design cycle to cover the full risk surface.
  </MatrixCell>
</MatrixGrid>

suggestions.content:
• BA builds a clickable experience in ~6 hours with direct client pairing.
  • Iterations focus on UI polish, so guardrails and scope boundaries fall through the cracks.
  • Front-end validation stands in for requirements, leaving others to reverse-engineer intent.

Pain points

  • No consistent artifact explaining in/out of scope for the prototype.
  • Prompt history is trapped in chat logs, hard to recreate or audit.
  • Client feedback is captured ad hoc so decisions get lost between sessions.
  • Prototype Scope Agreement with boundaries, lifespan, expectations.
  • Prompt Engineering Session Log for reproducibility + compliance.
  • Client Validation Capture Template tracking flows, feedback, sign-offs.

Example accelerators

  • In/out-of-scope checklist with disposal dates.
  • Prompt iteration table showing model, date, outcomes.
  • Meeting template with decisions, open questions, owners.

suggestions.content:
• Teams lean on LLMs to "review" prototype code without consistent criteria.
  • Production readiness discussions run without quantified evidence.
  • Technical debt is known qualitatively yet rarely captured in a structured artifact.

Risks

  • Subjective go/no-go decisions without weighted scoring.
  • Hidden security/compliance blockers surfacing late in regulated environments.
  • Estimation inflation when debt is discovered during hardening.
  • Multi-dimensional scorecard covering structure, security, testability, more.
  • Detailed assessment checklists per dimension for objective reviews.
  • Technical debt inventory with severity, effort, and release gating.

Example accelerators

  • Weighted scoring template with recommendation thresholds.
  • Checklist snippets aligned to OWASP, maintainability, scalability, etc.
  • Debt register linking each issue to remediation effort before production.

suggestions.content:
Code Structure (15%)

  • Separation of concerns
  • Consistent naming
  • File organization
  • No circular deps
  • Reasonable function sizes

Security Posture (20%)

  • Input validation
  • No hardcoded secrets
  • No SQL injection
  • No XSS vectors
  • Auth hooks ready

Error Handling (10%)

  • Try/catch around risky ops
  • User-friendly messages
  • Error logging
  • Graceful degradation
  • No silent failures

Testability (15%)

  • Unit-testable functions
  • Injectable dependencies
  • Isolated side effects
  • Mockable test data
  • Clear inputs/outputs

Scalability (10%)

  • No O(n²+) algorithms
  • Optimizable queries
  • Stateless where possible
  • Caching hooks available
  • Async patterns considered

Maintainability (15%)

  • Readable without comments
  • Complex logic documented
  • No magic numbers
  • Config externalized
  • Meaningful logging

Documentation (5%)

  • README exists
  • Setup instructions
  • API contracts defined
  • Architecture decisions noted

Dependency Health (10%)

  • Dependencies current
  • Vulnerabilities remediated
  • Licenses compatible
  • No unnecessary bloat

Overall Assessment

Each criterion is weighted based on impact to production readiness. Total score determines go/no-go recommendation.

Score: 85% Recommended

suggestions.content:
• Front-end behavior is being treated as the requirements baseline.
  • Non-functional requirements and client-specific standards are missing.
  • Teams need a consistent way to reverse-engineer intent from the prototype.
  • Reverse Engineering Requirements Document covering flows, rules, validation, NF needs.
  • Assumptions Register to track inferred decisions until validated with the client.
  • Non-functional requirements decoupled so performance, availability, security, etc., are explicit.

suggestions.content:
| Prototype Component | Production Component |
|---------------------|----------------------|
| Flask dev server | Gunicorn + Nginx / Cloud Run |
| Local file storage | S3 / Azure Blob / GCS |
| Hardcoded config | Environment variables / Secrets Manager |
| In-memory session | Redis / DB-backed sessions |
| Console logging | CloudWatch / Application Insights |
| No authentication | SSO integration (SAML/OIDC) |
| Sync processing | Queue-based async (for large files) |

suggestions.content:
**Options considered**
1. **Containerized (Docker + Kubernetes)** – scalable and portable; more complex for simple apps.
2. **Serverless (AWS Lambda + API Gateway)** – cost-effective and no servers; cold starts and 15-minute limits.
3. **PaaS (Heroku, Azure App Service)** – simple deployment; less control and potentially higher costs at scale.

suggestions.content:
| Activity | Optimistic | Likely | Pessimistic | Expected (PERT) |
|----------|------------|--------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Technical Debt Remediation** | | | | |
| Security fixes | 4h | 8h | 16h | 9h |
| Error handling | 2h | 4h | 8h | 4.3h |
| Configuration externalization | 2h | 4h | 6h | 4h |
| Logging implementation | 2h | 4h | 8h | 4.3h |
| **New Development** | | | | |
| SSO Integration | 8h | 16h | 32h | 17.3h |
| Production deployment config | 4h | 8h | 16h | 9h |
| **Testing** | | | | |
| Unit tests | 8h | 16h | 24h | 16h |
| Integration tests | 4h | 8h | 16h | 9h |
| Security testing | 4h | 8h | 12h | 8h |
| **Documentation** | | | | |
| Technical documentation | 4h | 8h | 12h | 8h |
| User documentation | 2h | 4h | 8h | 4.3h |
| **Deployment & DevOps** | | | | |
| CI/CD pipeline | 4h | 8h | 16h | 9h |
| Environment setup | 4h | 8h | 12h | 8h |
| **Buffer (20%)** | | | | ~22h |
| **TOTAL** | | | | **~132h** |

|--------|-------------|--------|
| **Prototype to Production Time** | Days from client prototype approval to production | < 4 weeks |
| **Rewrite Percentage** | % of prototype code replaced | < 40% |
| **Estimation Accuracy** | Actual effort / Estimated effort | 0.9 – 1.1 |
| **Client Satisfaction** | Survey score | > 4.5 / 5 |
| **Defects in Production** | Critical/high bugs in first 30 days | 0 |
| **Assessment Accuracy** | Did assessment predict issues correctly? | > 80% |

suggestions.content:
| Phase | Focus | Typical Duration | Key Output | Gate |
|-------|-------|------------------|------------|------|
| 0 | Qualify | Hours | Suitability matrix & viability proof | Gate 0 |
| 1 | Prototype | Hours | Validated prototype + scope dossier | Gate 1 |
| 2 | Assess | Days | Technical assessment & debt register | Gate 2 |
| 3 | Derive Requirements | Days | Requirements specification & assumptions log | Gate 3 |
| 4 | Design | Days | Architecture ADRs & integration blueprint | Gate 4 |
| 5 | Harden | Weeks | Tested, documented build ready for UAT | Gate 5 |
| 6 | Deliver | Days | Production release & runbook | Gate 6 |

suggestions.content:
1. **Process Ownership:** Who owns the vibe-to-production process—delivery methodology, solutions, or an AI enablement group?
2. **Tool Standardization:** Which AI tools are approved for vibe coding to ensure consistency and compliance?
3. **Training Requirements:** What training do BAs need for vibe coding and what do engineers need for assessment?
4. **Pricing Model Approval:** Does finance need to approve a new pricing model for vibe-coded production work?
5. **Quality Gate Authority:** Who can approve each gate, especially for regulated engagements?
6. **Liability Considerations:** What contractual implications exist when delivering AI-generated code?
7. **Intellectual Property:** Who owns prompts, prototypes, and derivative artifacts—do we need legal guidance?
8. **Resource Model:** Does the approach change staffing (e.g., shorter engagements with more senior talent)?
